comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.title: Non-religious folk magic
comments.content: Hello JMG. A couple of months ago, one of your readers here asked about removing verbal curses. You advised them to take a black candle, magically "download" the curse from the invisible worlds into the candle and let it burn. Although I haven't performed this ritual (haven't seen much of a need), I really like it in theory, and was wondering if you could recommend any books which contain sensible, non-religious folk magic like this. Hoodoo has good magic, but it's a little too religious for my taste. And none of the currently available books on candle magic have impressed me.

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content:
Folk magic is heavily religious, because atheism is a habit of the urban intelligentsia -- that is to say, not the people who practice folk magic! -- and religious people reliably do the smart thing and explore the magical dimensions of their religious faith as a way to make their magic more potent.

Thus you're pretty much out of luck. If you're willing to study folk magic as it actually exists -- that is to say, with the strong religious element it normally has -- and then adapt it to your own set of beliefs, that would be an option, but other than that, I'm not at all sure what to suggest.

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content: Hmm. But then, I know that you're against modifying magical systems that a person hasn't mastered. Is folk magic an exception to this rule? Can I, for example, take a book on Hoodoo and adapt it to my liking?

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content: Nope. That's why I said you'd have to study folk magic as it actually exists -- as in, work with it in theory and practice until you've got a good grasp of how it works -- and then adapt it. Alternatively, you could try to find somebody else who's sympathetic to your point of view and has a good solid grasp of the system, and could help walk you through the process.

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content: The second option sounds quite good to me. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content: Slightly different question: do you know of any resources for (or systems of) druidical or earth-based spirituality folk magic?

replies.title: Re: Non-religious folk magic
replies.content: Not yet, no. That's going to have to wait until somebody is willing to put in the hard work needed to master at least one traditional system of folk magic, on its own terms, and then rework it to fit a Druidical or earth-based framework.

comments.title: Drat!  More Curses!
comments.content:
Interesting, I have a "curse" question too. I'm not skilled enough to have a clue about verbal versus other types of curses.

I've been reading your writings about magic and civilization frequently (not 100%) for years -- but I haven't committed to any formal practice yet -- can you advise me how to detect (and hopefully counter) whether I might be under a curse in the first place? I don't recall you discussing curses to any real depth on your blogs, only mentioning them in passing. I'll have to bare some personal details, but I'll try to keep it as succinct as I can. I'd like to think I'm more interested in _screening out the possibility_ of a curse, than "blaming" a curse for my own problems.

My understanding of what you're writing about magic leads me to believe that "random chance" doesn't play as much of a role in people's lives as people think; "luck" (good/bad) is not as arbitrary as we often believe. It's about subtle pattern recognition versus (intentional/unintentional) blindness.

In the first three months of this year I had several "unlucky" medical problems on top of each other, starting with a very sudden, out of the blue, near-fatal attack of Diabetic Keto-acidosis despite many years of eating fairly healthy and simply. Pretty much soon as I got out of the hospital I stepped on a used syringe at the beach (turned out to be nothing, but that was a stressful two months of waiting for AIDS and Hep tests) and subsequently was diagnosed with a double hernia despite little physical exertion. This comes on top of years of slow financial degradation; it's been 20 years since I've had a job that didn't turn into a nightmare, financially _and_ emotionally, after initially seeming promising. Never much luck with romance in my life, either, FWIW.

I find it unlikely that I'm the victim of intentional work against me by a practitioner (the "witch's curse" in pop fantasy). Obviously there could be a million ways that I create a pattern of self-sabotage ("bad luck") for myself, and I try my best to work on that with stoicism and persistence and your blog advice. But if I (as an outsider) understand your magical system correctly, I _could_ have attracted the interest of extra-personal malign forces -- perhaps as a result of my occasional nonserious dabble in magic: reading Crowley type stuff as an adolescent, or trying your TV/Pen Knife experiment lately, stuff like that on and off for a long time.

Could there be a hereditary curse, in your magical system? Both my parents seemed to have had more than their share of financial un-success and "random" medical ailments. I suppose that all three of our stories could be simply a feature of living in the twilight of a dying Empire. I'm quite aware that I enjoy historical "good luck" as a modern American middle class citizen: from drinking as much water as I want without a thought towards dysentery... to, visiting multiple foreign countries, blithely free from any orders to kill people while there. Still, within the context of the American middle class -- as Charlie Brown said, I never seem to get a break.

In short (if it's not too conceited of me), what magical advice would you give to Charlie Brown if he described his life to you? Charlie Brown may carry a poor pessimistic attitude around with him, but the fact that "by co-incidence" he ends up with rocks in his Trick-or-Treat bag while his friends get candy does indicate that forces of some sort are arrayed against him. If I were to seek specific advice on this from a local practitioner -- any tips (amplifying stuff I've read on your blog in the past) about finding sincere ones and avoiding charlatans?

If this is a deep enough subject for a separate column, I understand... just please don't leave me hanging long!

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Hmm. The magical advice I'd give to Charlie Brown would probably not be welcomed by him, and may not be welcomed by you. If you look over his cartoon life, you might notice that a huge number of his misfortunes were self-generated -- how many times did he go ahead and try to kick the football Lucy held for him, even though he knew she was going to snatch it away? How many times did he fly his kite near the kite-eating tree? As the saying goes, "the one common factor in all your problems is you." Except for situations where there's good reason to think you've come in for the attention of a hostile practitioner, it's most likely that what's going on is a combination of the ordinary troubles of human existence, on the one hand, and the effects of various dysfunctional habits and attitudes, on the other.

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Yeah, I figured that was the most likely possibility.  Thanks for your service & dedication to your readership!

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Have you done any sort of divination on this? Might be useful.

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Good idea, but I am also inexperienced at divination.  If I _did_ happen to be under some kind of malign influence (self or external), I wonder if it would taint the divination.  I would be willing to divulge a few personal details if it would help somebody else do a reading on me.  I downloaded a computer version of "Geomancy", which our benevolent host favors, and I've toyed with it a bit, but to my inexpert eye I seem to get a lot of weird contradictory readings.

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: For whatever it's worth, I'm relatively inexperienced, but I get consistently situation-appropriate responses from the I ching. One perk is that it's such an established presence with a substantial body of interpretation that it can be more like a voice of experience clothed in the symbolic than purely a symbolic image that you have to interpret on your own. I favor a translation that hearkens back to mythic imagery but the old standby, by Wilhelm, is held in high regard and is widely available.

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Good recommendation, thanks!

replies.title: Re: Drat!  More Curses!
replies.content: Besides thanking you for your consideration, I'd also like to point out -- while it's no fun to hear that -- from a certain perspective, it's good news.  It's nice to have at least one expert opinion saying that malign magical extra-personal forces do not typically strike a "muggle" (non-practitioner) arbitrarily.  That's one worry I can now cross off my list!  ;-)

comments.title: Bankruptcy and Karma
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have an acquaintance who has both student loan debt, which she does not plan to pay back, as well as a business bankruptcy.

The student loan was for a degree that proved worthless, and the bankruptcy was perfectly legal.

Karmically, is either debt still owed? I’d be very interested in your take on this.

Thanks for hosting Magic Monday!

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content: I'm not one of the Lords of Karma, and I don't know the person in question, so my likelihood of providing an accurate answer is probably no better than yours!

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content: Ha!  Thanks for the reminder that it’s complicated.  😊

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content:
The way I understand individual karma, it's between individuals, not between an individual and a group of people, whether incorporated or not.

There may be individuals on the other side of the bankruptcy that ce owes karma to, or they may be part of a karmic repayment. There's no way to tell without knowing more detail.

There's also collective karma, and that may be in play with the student loans, but if so it wouldn't matter if ce planned to repay or not.

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content:
Although there may be a mystical dimension to karma, it's often enough to understand it in more practical terms:

1. Student loans have been collateralized and sold as assets to various economic agents, who in term use them to underwrite further economic activities. If enough people don't pay back their students loans, then a similar situation to the one that occurred in 2008 with delinquent mortgages will happen again. This is the karmic effect of not paying back student loans along with other actions (such as offering loans on such terrible terms in the first place).
2. The business bankruptcy was presumably undertaken to discharge debts incurred in the course of running a business. Those debts were owed to other businesses, which were counting on the debts being repayed as part of their revenue. As a result of not receiving this revenue, those businesses will not be able to pursue various opportunities they otherwise would have. People may get laid off. Some businesses on the margin of profitability may go out of business entirely. These are the karmic effects of discharging debt via bankruptcy. (The legality of the bankruptcy guarantees that things like civil court trials are not part of the karmic effects for doing so.)

To understand the rightness or wrongness of actions, it usually suffices to understand the quotidian "real-world" effects of those actions.

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content: Or.... a promise was made to repay a debt. Your word is either worth something or it isn't.

replies.title: Re: Bankruptcy and Karma
replies.content: But that "isn't" something might be more than nothing.

Dear JMG
You say that one should do the LBRP daily. What would happen if instead you replaced it with the lesser hexagram ritual? Would you get the same benefits?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. It's a different ritual with different effects.

Would one do the lbrp with the lesser hexagram ritual together instead?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Just practicing the LRH risks imbalance, since it's a banishing of Saturn, and banishing just one planet while doing nothing with the others is not necessarily a good thing.

What I'd recommend instead is that you consider doing a series of simple planetary workings, one each week, working through the planets from the Moon up to Saturn and back again. Open with the LBRP and LBRH, purify, consecrate, and circumambulate in the usual way, proclaim the temple open, then do the greater Hexagram ritual of the planet you're invoking, then call upon the planet's energies to strengthen its positive aspects in you -- for example, patience and perseverancefrom Saturn, justice and generosity from Jupiter, and so on. (You can make this invocation as simple or as complex as you wish.) Then thank the planetary energies, do the greater Hexagram ritual to banish, then purify, consecrate, circumambulate in reverse, and close with LBRH and LBRP. By the time you've done the whole sequence once, you'll have the LBRH down pat; by the time you've done the whole sequence seven times -- which is what I'd recommend; if you do one a week you'll complete that in under a year -- not only will you have the entire set of Hexagram rituals down cold, you'll have self-initiated into a basic relationship with the planetary energies, and likely benefited hugely from the process as well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That is a really goog idea, thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you! That's the way I did it in my Golden Dawn days -- every time I started work on a new set of rituals, I'd figure out some relatively straightforward ritual form and then do a sequence of self-initiation workings using it. It worked very well, both as a way to learn the rituals and as a way to get used to invoking the various magical energies and working with them.

comments.title: Ecstatic Body Postures
comments.content:
Good morning Mr. Greer,

You say the old, original druid practices are lost and what we have now is a revival which is being drawn from the insights of today's practitioners

Are you acquainted with Felicitas D. Goodman's research on ecstatic body postures, have you read her book "Where The Spirits Ride The Wind"?

I wonder if these techniques wouldn't be a way to reclaim old lost rituals. Kind of a window or 'time tunnel'to the past...

replies.title: Re: Ecstatic Body Postures
replies.content:
Except it's not a window into the past. We have no way of knowing whether or not our nervous systems will respond to those postures in exactly the same way that the nervous systems of people ten thousand years ago did.

Trust me, all kinds of gimmicks have been tried in an attempt to recover scraps of the old Druid practices. When mediums were fashionable, people tried to get the spirits of old Druids in seances; when hypnotic regression became fashionable, people tried to remember their past lives as Druids; Goodman's methods could doubtless become the basis of another such attempt. What happens in every case is that what gets "recovered" is a precise fit to the beliefs and expectations of the people doing the "recovering." Since that's the case, we might as well be honest and go ahead and invent things.

replies.title: Re: Ecstatic Body Postures
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

I had not heard of this book or this author before.

I do not have a daily meditation or energy-working practice. FWIW, I have found that my most reliable method for achieving middle-level trance states is to move around or dance, alone or with others, in a Wiccan-style cast circle, while reciting or listening to good poetry or singing a chant with a good tune or good words. Under those circumstances, I sometimes get messages on the order of "Do this next," "Make this gesture repeatedly," "Stamp your feet," "Squat and open up your lower energy centers to earth energy," "Cast intentional energy into the fire/water/some object," "Whistle," and that sort of thing.

These messages come through my body and my mind at the same time. They are certainly informed by what I have read about folk magical practices, and I have never received one that is an utter surprise. It is possible that they are simply my subconscious making associations and bringing them to my attention, but there is not a thick boundary between that process and Messages from the Ancestors, which is what it feels like when it happens. I do whatever is directed immediately unless it would be disruptive or distracting to the ritual. Unlike some information received while in a light trance, I often remember these messages when the ritual is over, and incorporate them into my practice.

replies.title: Re: Ecstatic Body Postures
replies.content: Goodman's "Where the Spirits Ride the Wind" was a fascinating read, and she may have been on the trail of something historically important.  For a while she ran a center for experimenting with the trance-work postures she had documented, the Cuyamungue Institute.  Nana Nauwald co-authored with Goodman a book on that work, "Ecstatic Trance: A Workbook, New Ritual Body Postures" (2003).  I stopped keeping up with its work after Goodman's own death in 2005, but the Institute still operates and has a web-site.  The results as of 2005 didn't strike me as very conclusive.

replies.title: Re: Ecstatic Body Postures
replies.content: Also, JMG got there before me, but there was definitely a lot of suggestion and expectation at work in the results I saw reported while I was following Goodman;s work.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
If you really agree that it's a waste of time to interpret a geomantic chart based on answers from a table, why are you even asking this? Learn the meanings of the figures -- there are only sixteen of them, it's not that hard -- and you won't have this kind of problem.

Cauda Draconis for both witnesses, yielding Populus for judge, means that something is coming to an end -- that's what the figure means -- on a collective level, affecting you and other people. Maybe what it's saying is that the life you're living -- along with most other people in this society -- isn't fit for a badly behaved dog, and it's time for you to do something else with your life.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Alright
Suppose I deserved that.

But I'm not taking the table's description as the ultimate truth.

I tried to go beyond its shallow message when I asked your opinion about some deeper truth that might be hidden in that cruel statement.

I guess what I really wanted to know is:

What made someone carve that judgement's evil in all description?

Could you shine a light?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Seriously, the tables are garbage. It's not just that they're shallow -- they're wrong. Really, seriously wrong. They're the reason why geomancy dropped out of common use -- the tables make it look so easy, and people stopped taking the time to learn the meanings of the figures, then wondered why their readings failed to make accurate predictions.

I don't know who made up the tables, but whoever it was clearly didn't know the first thing about practical geomantic divination. Throw the tables away, learn the figures and their meanings, and you can start getting clear readings that give you accurate information.

comments.title: On Elementals, Fae, and Humans
comments.content:
I think it is better to post this here than as a reply to an open post at the main blog.

I have a theory that some elementals, fairies, and humans are part of a continuum. Roughly, elementals evolve into fae, and those into humans, in what is not a one way path up. Thinking on what would be needed for this to work may provide explanations for a number of features in fairy folklore.

For starters, in principle it is not difficult to think of fairies as upgraded elementals. Add the missing elements to an elemental and you have a fay. But an elemental of a given type is blind to and incompatible with the other three elements. A path to connect an elemental with these would be fairies or humans with elemental imbalances. Hybrids may be way easier to form in discarnate realms than in our physical level. In case of contacts with an elemental of a single type, a human starts to develop an imbalance, in essence becoming an elemental by shedding the other elements away. But where the elements that the human lost would go to? I think they are not destroyed; instead, they merge with the elemental, which for all intents becomes a human soul, while the former human self goes to the elemental realm.

In less extreme cases, the elemental will become "more of a human," so to say. As such, the elemental becomes a fairy. Contacts with the fae also have the same risk of making a human "being sucked into Fairyland to never return." In the case of the infamous fairy food, I always thought of it as a formula that triggers the first death while preventing the second death.

Here we have two things explained, why we have so many encounters with these beings (there is an affinity), and how humans can become captives of Fairyland and the elemental realm. I want to stress that I believe the difference between fays and elementals is more of degree than kind, which would explain why it is difficult to tell them apart. It is also of interest that this provides a way for a human self to be downgraded into a fairy or an elemental. I always thought that the narrative of the one way path up into Gwynfydd reeks of eternal progress. This possibility of fall also leads to another feature of folklore, that sees the faeries as fallen angels. That might be the way a mind trained in the ways of Christ would understand and perceive a fallen human. Others would equate the fairies with the dead, that is, with what was perceived as former humans.

Now let's consider another broad folklore theme, "fae are in a permanent state of decay, and they need contact and intercourse with humans to inject new life into their faltering bloodlines." If we are dealing with a cousins continuum that would explain a lot in both ways, of problems that can arise from this (large servings of taboo included free of charge), and how the inherently unstable fae would benefit from communion with humans. Fae and humans hybrids would be much more stable, and highly desired.

The failures in Fae glamour illusions, where there is always a telling flaw, may reflect our perception of them as incomplete humans.

Another thing to ponder is that "contact" can occur in many degrees; for example, as possession. Or changelings. The higher self of a baby has a looser connection to the physical body than an adult, and hostile takeovers are a possibility. I guess Dion Fortune would describe these as the work of Left Hand Path fays.

Speaking of good old Violet, I suggest everyone to read chapter seven of her Psychic Self-Defence book, "The Pathology of Non-Human Contacts," with this "theory" in mind. Actually, my own reading of her book a couple of days ago was the final block that made me connect all these pieces together.

To end this as a question, what do you think about it?

replies.content: Hmm. It's at least worth exploring.

replies.title: Re: Spiritual practice
replies.content: The basic thesis of ecological spirituality is that human beings in industrial societies are like plants that have been uprooted, and kept more or less alive by having their roots soaked in water dosed with cheap commercial nutrient mixes. We are only half alive because we lack a living connection with the world of nature. The point of a ecological spirituality is to become fully alive, to participate richly in the life of the biosphere on all its levels, including the nonphysical ones, and thus to heal the "sickness unto death" that pervades contemporary industrial culture and its inmates.

replies.title: Re: Twin flames
replies.content:
It's another version of the old "soul mate" theory, which mostly functions as a pick-up line in New Age-themed singles bars. As Dion Fortune pointed out, it's really quite remarkable how reliably somebody's "twin soul" turns out to be married to someone else. No, I don't find it sound or useful.

I was speaking, in the comment you mention, about ways of training the will. If you crave something, and you have the ability to get it, one effective way to train the will is to deliberately not get it, and don't tell anyone. The latter is crucial; an embarrassing number of people turn self-discipline into an opportunity to posture in front of others.

I'm reminded of a guy I knew many years ago who was doing the faux-Buddhist self-discipline thing, complete with the classic no-ego ego trip ("I'm more egoless than you are!"). He went so far as to boast (in a very humble tone of voice, of course) that nobody in the group he was addressing could name an austerity too severe for him to do. I said, "I know one." He asked me to tell him, and I told him to gain five pounds and keep it on for a year. He lost his cool completely, because of course he realized what I was saying -- that the one thing he was utterly unwilling to give up was his vanity.

This is why perfect silence about the training of your will is so important. Let nobody know what you're doing -- and that in itself will help you build a powerful will.

(Deborah Bender)

I'm going to go where JMG rightly refuses to tread, and you can draw the obvious conclusion about my level of wisdom.

I am a casual student of Western herbalism--not an experienced practitioner like violetcabra. One thing I learned in my introductory studies is that the European herbalist tradition classifies plants by qualities. The most important qualities are wet/dry and cold/hot. If you have a cold constitution, you might be able to bring it a little more into balance by eating and drinking in moderation herbs that are hot --for example, drinking ginger tea and eating foods with hot spices and chili peppers in them.

You could look up lists of cold and hot herbs in a book on Western herbalism, and if you find that you are ingesting cold herbs every day, cut that back to a couple of times a week.

If you stick to plants that you have eaten/drunk before without ill effects, do this in moderation and pay attention to how you feel immediately afterward and over the next twelve hours or so, you can figure out if it has any effect at all.

If it makes a difference, you might look for a good clinical herbalist of the European-based variety (Asian herbalists uses different plants and different classification systems) and they might consult with you and and suggest a custom tea blend or a tincture that is suitable for a person of your constitution.

replies.title: Re: ogham sticks and breathing
replies.content:
1) Breathe in until your lungs are full. Then try to sniff in a little more air. Can you do this without anything shifting in your throat? comments.title: words effecting events
comments.content:
I am a violinist, composer, and sometimes lyricist. Years ago I experienced a prolonged period where it seemed as if my written words would spring to life. A simple example is that after having written into song the line “fallen to my knees...” I experienced some freakish accidents, tearing one kneecap off, and a compound fracture below the other knee. I became so paranoid about it that my writing was paralyzed – for years. (Even now, I feel uneasy about writing the previous sentence.)

I’ve approached thinking about it from many different angles: It’s just my pattern recognition in overdrive – Words ARE Spells – or, Life is Just Like That. The perspective that has rung most true for me is that the combined force of intention (whether conscious or not), emotion and clarity of words will call forth a sort of elemental life form.

I’d be grateful for your and any readers thoughts on this. More specifically my question is: What is your perspective on words becoming reality, and if it’s a genuine aspect of reality then what lends to some words carrying more force than others?

The topic is relevant for me now as I, with two other musicians, have begun live broadcasting the music we’re working with every month on the new moon. The one this Wednesday includes several songs that have strong lyrics of social commentary that have me pondering my question on a larger scale. Any feedback would be most welcome.

replies.title: Re: words effecting events
replies.content: It's possible for any intention made with enough concentration and force to have magical effects. The usual way of dealing with that among trained operative mages is to decide in advance how you want each intention to manifest, and to concentrate on that as you write or speak the words.

replies.title: Re: words effecting events
replies.content:
JMG, thank you.

If this isn’t appropriate to post, or not connected enough to my original question I understand. But I’ve been having a slowly growing epiphany around this topic, and am excited about it.

We’ve been approaching the live broadcasts I mentioned as New Moon meditations, playing works in progress, not necessarily polished pieces, while sending them into the airwaves of the interweb. While we hone in on the tech required to simultaneously record and broadcast, our listeners have been deliberately limited to friends (and whomever might come across us by chance).

I’d like to invite any of your readers here to join us in the process as listeners/meditators/intention setters. This month the theme is “The Cosmic VaudeVillain Show.” The intention could well be expressed from some lines in one of the songs written by my partner:


“I am not interested in the conforming society
I am interested in the Extraordinary Humanity
The Ordinary of Nature

One is well informed of insanity
Indoctrination! Rearranged words
Of preferred brainwashed education!
Hell, those institutions, along with
The bankers, church and state
Will tell me they paved the way...
With what – asphalt?

I guess we'll just have to walk a new path today.”


I’m visualizing a monthly ritual of music and intention – distinct from performer/audience – where the listening influences the performance (ask any musician, and they’ll tell you this is what happens in a live performance anyway, especially when it’s not all scripted beforehand). I’m intrigued with trying this even when participants are in different locations, but synchronized in time.

It could be something like the Trump binding spell, but NOT destructive or harmful, and doesn't require official magical techniques. The intention here is on creative, nourishing forces. At the very least it could be a feedback loop of inspiration….?

We’ll be broad-Casting on the New Moon tomorrow at 9:00pm MST at tendervishes.net.


Many thanks,

Mira Fortuna

comments.title: Astral realms
comments.content:
Maybe I already asked you this question at the "main" blog, but I don´t remember the answer, so here we go again.

In occultism, Earth is part of a broader "system" (so to speak) also including etheric, astral and mental realms. Are these realms connected to one planet only (in this case our Earth), or do they also reach out into space - other planets, deep space, etc? Or does each planet have its own astral and mental spheres?

In principle, could "aliens" from another world reach Earth through the astral, or is this impossible?

If I understand them correctly, Theosophists suggest that each planet is on an evolutionary trajectory all its own, while Steiner suggested it was the entire solar system which evolved (while being attacked by "Sorath", a demon from another part of the cosmos entirely).

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Astral realms
replies.content: Different systems of occult philosophy make different claims about this. To my mind, it's not really that interesting, as there doesn't seem to be any reason to think that I might have to deal with astral aliens any time soon...

comments.title: Psychoanalysis
comments.content: I vaguely remember reading that in the early 20th century, when Freudianism was fashionable, some magician wrote that no one should take up magic without first being psychoanalyzed.  Now that paychoanalysis is passe, what should the prospective magician do in lieu of it?

replies.title: Re: Psychoanalysis
replies.content: Start practicing magic!

replies.title: Re: Psychoanalysis
replies.content: Israel Regardie said that.

replies.title: Re: Psychoanalysis
replies.content: I know, but that doesn't mean he was right. He learned magic at a time when psychoanalysis was all the rage, and a lot of people -- Dion Fortune among them -- believed that a fusion between magic and depth psychology was the wave of the future. What they forgot was that every wave eventually breaks and rolls back out to sea...

Hi JMG,

Is it a bad idea to pray to a monotheistic God if one is not monotheistic (or affiliated with that God) oneself? I occasionally get prayer requests for Christian friends that I would like to honor, but they would object to my praying to deities other than the Christian God on their behalf; however, I'm not sure that He would welcome prayers from a non-Christian, and would not like to draw harmful attention onto myself or those for whom I am praying.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd recommend taking the question to the only one who can answer it, i.e., the god in question. It's not necessary to be a monotheist to believe that Christians do in fact pray to an actual god, after all. Try praying to the Christian god and asking him whether he would be willing to hear prayers that your Christian friends ask you to make, or not, and then pay attention to the response you get.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hmm. The problem is, I never got any noticeable response to prayer from the Christian God when I was trying to be Christian, and still don't seem to. Perhaps I have been too vague--it feels more like praying to "the universe" than a particular god, maybe because I figured he was the only god out there for so long, if anybody was. I did one time get a string of synchronicities when I asked for a sign. I will try being more specific and see what I can manage. Thanks for your response!

And a further update on the anti-glamour experiment: my phone habits have become more mindless again after removing the pins. Excessive ebooks, an unfortunate trip into the bowels of Reddit. I am working to consciously counter this now that I'm aware of it. I haven't replaced the pins for fear of finishing off my phone entirely, but I am personally convinced that the pins had a beneficial effect and that removing them interrupted it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi Jen,

If I may.

I came a long way from completely denying Christianity to addressing my prayers to the Christian God daily. Christian and a few other gods and entities as a part of my daily devotion practice.

For me the main stumbling block was to get beyond this feeling of Christian God as some abstract and very impersonal figure that I didn't believe in anyway. My gateway was through a very personal image of Jesus that gave me and him a medium we could communicate through.

As it sounds, any side of the holy trinity can be addressed separately and different people would resonate better with one of them. If you had any practices that felt authentic in relation to The Great Spirit, praying to The Holy Spirit might be a thing.

Hope it helps,
Ganesh

comments.title: Lunar difficulties
comments.content:
I’ve noticed that the times of the new moon and the full moon tend to be rough patches for me. This usually takes the form of an emotional blowout, typically involving conflict with a family member (mother or sister) over some dumb McGuffin. Is there anything I can do to forestall this sort of thing?

On Sundays and occasionally on other days I perform an invocation of the Sun, as described in your book “Circles of Power,” placing Hellenic deities at the cardinal points and above & below. Would it be a bad idea to circulate the light after this ceremony, before closing out with the Tau Cross? Should that be reserved for banishing rituals - and if so, do they necessarily have to be those of the Pentagrams?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Lunar difficulties
replies.content:
1) You might consider doing some lunar workings on the first and last quarters, asking for a more balanced response to the lunar energies.

2) It sounds to me as though you're doing a mashup of Circles of Power and The Druid Magic Handbook, which I don't recommend, and of the solar invocation in the former book with the Sphere of Protection from the latter, which I recommend even less. If that's so, yes, it would probably be a bad idea -- certainly I wouldn't do that.

comments.title: Theurgical Temple and Practice
comments.content:
I’m considering creating a body of devotional images of Hellenic deities and nature spirits. I would like to assemble for them a Temple designed for theurgical purposes, characterized by an idiosyncratic architectonic style. The sort of operations I have in mind for such a Temple are those described by John Opsopaus in his “Summary of Pythagorean Theology,” particularly the part on Theurgy:

http://opsopaus.com/OM/BA/ETP/V.html

The one that most interests me is “7. Telestikê, or Ensoulment.” Or rather, it is the operation to which I feel best suited at present.

Several years ago I asked Richard Reidy, a Kemetic revivalist and scholar, about this practice, and he warned me that it is not something to be undertaken lightly. Having “animated” such an image, one must fulfill a priestly responsibility to it, or the deity in question is likely to send you something other than blessings, as Dr. Reidy put it. So that is something I would need to think carefully about.

The practice that John Opsopaus calls Theurgic Ascent (No. 10) strikes me as a good preparation for death. Not that I don’t wish to delay this as much as possible, but as Aldous Huxley wrote, one’s a fool if one doesn’t prepare for it.

Clearly I will need to make the decision about whether to undertake such a body of work. But I’d be interested in your opinion. Is this a mad enterprise, or one that is likely to bear good fruit?

I’ll be doing some form of divination about it, of course; probably the Tarot. But my skills there are very basic.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Theurgical Temple and Practice
replies.content: That's not a question I can answer for you. You yourself have to decide whether those are responsibilities you're ready to accept.

replies.title: Re: Theurgical Temple and Practice
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

I second what Richard Reidy said, and add that if you are thinking of ensouling a physical object, you ought to make arrangements for passing it on to some person or some group that can be counted on to look after it and care for it after you die, just as a responsible person would do before adopting a long-lived intelligent animal like a parrot.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Just realized the date in time.


So I have been doing some community events recently, and we are currently planning a Midsummer gathering for the 23rd. Mostly this is not a magical matter, so much as standard event planning. But there is one magical element...

Out my East facing window there is a tall plume of smoke from the 416 fire in Durango and the Burro Fire to it's North; the whole area is a tinder box, and we are in a serious drought. How ever weather models are implying that an early and strong monsoon season is more likely than average. Generally July is when the monsoons start to drop their most lovely 4 pm scattered showers.

Bringing those points together, the event host and I were talking about having a rain ceremony. I leaned gently against a rain dance unless we could get a trained representative from an tribe with a history of connections to the appropriate forces; under the assumption that (how to put this?) who ever in on the other end of the call for rain might just have 'caller ID', let's just say. Still though the idea of getting folks together to pray for the dearly needed monsoons seems to be a self evidently good idea. I am trying to think out how best to do that, and asking if a thought occurs to you which might be worth minding?

Since on the level of ideology and story keeping my community is very diverse, but on the mood about the rain we are very tightly of one heart, I want it to be action that doesn't put off one ideology over another. The leading notion is that we will have a small pool at the event for folks to dip into to cool off. Then we invited folks to bring their herbs which are expiring this season to cast into the water (there by making room for the coming harvest of fresh herbs, consider it an act of faith there will be such a harvest). This body wash herbal bath will then be carried and given to the various trees on the property with wished for rain.

That's the basic notion, any thoughts on some details that might help or hinder our efforts?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Make sure you include appropriate prayers to the spirits of the place, asking their permission and help for the working; if you typically do offerings to spirits and deities, some to the spirits of the place would be well worth including.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

First time caller here. My questions has to do with charting one's "life path"- for lack of a better term. During one of your interviews a couple years ago( on the Leak Project for nearly 3 hours) I believe you said that you would give readings to people, and through said readings you could figure out which path a person should go down.

What do you think is the best way for a someone to suss out such a thing ? Would you recommend tarot, palm reading, or something else entirely? And if so, how could one tell who would be a legitimate person to go to? For example, if I wanted to find a good burger place( not to be too prosaic with my comparisons, but that's the best analogy that I could come up with) I could find review sites for such a thing. Sorry if this is a bit of a noob question, but I am rather new to the Occult, and you're one of the only people I would trust to give the straight answer in regards to such things. I ask because I'm at a bit of a crossroads in life, and was wondering if the Occult can work as a guide to what path to take. Thank you, in advance, for any advice that you can offer.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't think that was me -- it's been a long time since I've done readings for other people, and I hope I've never been arrogant enough to think that I can tell people what to do with their lives!

I honestly don't recommend trying to chart out a "life path" in advance. There's an old saying that the best way to make the gods laugh is to tell them your plans! If you face an important choice in your life right now, take the time to sort out the options -- many people find that it helps to write the options down, and then write out the pros and cons of each choice. If that doesn't sort things out -- very often it does -- remember that you have a higher self, a spiritual essence that endures from life to life and has a clearer sense of the overall pattern of things than your lower self (the self you're conscious of most of the time). Turn your attention to your higher self and ask it for insight into the decision. That may come quickly or slowly, but it'll come.

Last Magic Monday you mentioned that leaving a book open in the sun or burning frankincense and wafting the smoke over the pages could cleanse the book. Why does frankincense have the capacity to do this? Can another insence be used?

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Different incenses have different magical effects. Frankincense has a strong cleansing and purifying effect, so no, you can't use another incense and get the same effect. That's like thinking you can substitute a cup of salt for the cup of sugar in a cake recipe, and still get something tasty!
